structured.summary: This paper investigates whether large pre-trained text-only language models (LMs), such as GPT-2 and GPT-3, can learn to ground linguistic concepts (e.g., spatial directions, cardinal directions, colors) to their corresponding representations in grounded conceptual spaces using only a few examples. The study finds that while smaller models struggle, the largest GPT-3 model can successfully generalize to unseen grounded concepts and worlds, suggesting that large LMs learn a conceptual structure that can be efficiently mapped to grounded representations.

strengths.point: Novel investigation into grounding text-only LMs without retraining
strengths.evidence: The paper explores teaching pre-trained LMs to ground concepts via in-context learning without parameter updates (§2.2, §3).

strengths.point: Comprehensive experimental design across multiple grounded domains
strengths.evidence: Experiments cover spatial terms, cardinal directions, and color terms with carefully constructed textual representations of grounded worlds (§2.3).

strengths.point: Use of isomorphic rotated worlds as a control to rule out memorization
strengths.evidence: The paper introduces rotated and random worlds to test if models rely on memorization or conceptual mapping (§2.4, §3.1).

strengths.point: Detailed error analysis and grounding distance metric
strengths.evidence: The authors analyze model errors qualitatively and quantitatively using grounding distance to assess how close incorrect predictions are (§3.3).

weaknesses.point: Limited scope of grounded concepts due to text-only input restriction
weaknesses.evidence: The study focuses on domains that can be faithfully represented in text (e.g., grid worlds, RGB codes), excluding more complex sensory or visual concepts (§4 Discussion).

weaknesses.point: Lack of evaluation on more complex or real-world grounding tasks
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments are limited to synthetic grid worlds and color spaces, which may not generalize to richer multimodal grounding (§4 Discussion).

weaknesses.point: Potential influence of pretraining data overlap not fully controlled
weaknesses.evidence: Although rotated worlds control for memorization, the paper acknowledges that some grounding examples might appear in training data, and the training corpus is not publicly available (§2.4, Appendix F).

weaknesses.point: Smaller models perform poorly, limiting applicability to only very large models
weaknesses.evidence: Results show that only the largest GPT-3 model (175B parameters) achieves reasonable grounding performance, while smaller GPT-2 models fail (§3.1, §3.2).

weaknesses.point: Evaluation metrics rely on substring matches which may overestimate correctness
weaknesses.evidence: Top-1 accuracy counts substring matches as correct, which might inflate scores for partial or approximate answers (§2.5, Appendix C).

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on how to extend approach to non-textual inputs
weaknesses.evidence: The paper notes the limitation of text-only inputs but does not provide concrete methods for adapting models to multimodal inputs (§4 Discussion).

weaknesses.point: No ablation or analysis on prompt design or number of examples
weaknesses.evidence: While prompt sizes are mentioned, there is limited exploration of how prompt composition affects grounding performance (§2.2, Appendix D).

questions.question: How sensitive is the grounding performance to the choice and number of in-context examples in the prompt?
questions.motivation: Understanding this would clarify the data efficiency and robustness of the proposed grounding approach.

questions.question: Can the authors provide insights or preliminary results on extending this approach to multimodal inputs beyond text-based representations?
questions.motivation: Addressing this would help assess the feasibility of applying the method to more complex grounded concepts.

questions.question: To what extent do the models rely on memorized associations from pretraining data versus truly learning the conceptual mapping?
questions.motivation: Clarifying this would strengthen claims about conceptual grounding rather than memorization.

questions.question: Have the authors considered evaluating the approach on more naturalistic or noisy grounded data, such as real images or sensor readings represented textually?
questions.motivation: This would test the generalizability and practical applicability of the method.

critique_points.point_001: Limited scope of grounded concepts due to text-only input restriction (The study focuses on domains that can be faithfully represented in text (e.g., grid worlds, RGB codes), excluding more complex sensory or visual concepts (§4 Discussion).)
critique_points.point_002: Lack of evaluation on more complex or real-world grounding tasks (Experiments are limited to synthetic grid worlds and color spaces, which may not generalize to richer multimodal grounding (§4 Discussion).)
critique_points.point_003: Potential influence of pretraining data overlap not fully controlled (Although rotated worlds control for memorization, the paper acknowledges that some grounding examples might appear in training data, and the training corpus is not publicly available (§2.4, Appendix F).)
critique_points.point_004: Smaller models perform poorly, limiting applicability to only very large models (Results show that only the largest GPT-3 model (175B parameters) achieves reasonable grounding performance, while smaller GPT-2 models fail (§3.1, §3.2).)
critique_points.point_005: Evaluation metrics rely on substring matches which may overestimate correctness (Top-1 accuracy counts substring matches as correct, which might inflate scores for partial or approximate answers (§2.5, Appendix C).)
critique_points.point_006: Limited discussion on how to extend approach to non-textual inputs (The paper notes the limitation of text-only inputs but does not provide concrete methods for adapting models to multimodal inputs (§4 Discussion).)
critique_points.point_007: No ablation or analysis on prompt design or number of examples (While prompt sizes are mentioned, there is limited exploration of how prompt composition affects grounding performance (§2.2, Appendix D).)
critique_points.point_008: How sensitive is the grounding performance to the choice and number of in-context examples in the prompt? (Understanding this would clarify the data efficiency and robustness of the proposed grounding approach.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the authors provide insights or preliminary results on extending this approach to multimodal inputs beyond text-based representations? (Addressing this would help assess the feasibility of applying the method to more complex grounded concepts.)
critique_points.point_010: To what extent do the models rely on memorized associations from pretraining data versus truly learning the conceptual mapping? (Clarifying this would strengthen claims about conceptual grounding rather than memorization.)
critique_points.point_011: Have the authors considered evaluating the approach on more naturalistic or noisy grounded data, such as real images or sensor readings represented textually? (This would test the generalizability and practical applicability of the method.)
